mnemonics.title: Try-Catch-Flow
mnemonics.content: T-C-T: Think Closely About - Remember: Try contains risky code, Catch handles errors, Throw signals problems

mnemonics.title: Catch Order Rule
mnemonics.content: S.P.E.C.I.A.L: Specific catch blocks must come Before Ellipsis (...) Catch ALways Last

mnemonics.title: Exception Hierarchy
mnemonics.content: E.L.R.B: Exception is base, Logic error, Runtime error, Bad exceptions (alloc, cast)

acronyms.usage: Pattern where resource is acquired in constructor and released in destructor, works well with exception handling
Specifiers that guarantee a function won't throw exceptions, enables compiler optimizations
Contains standard exception classes that can be caught and handled in user programs

visualTips.visualization: Draw a flowchart: try box with arrow to catch boxes. If exception thrown, arrow diverts to matching catch. No match = arrow to terminate.
Draw an inverted tree: std::exception at top, two branches: logic_error and runtime_error, then leaves: bad_alloc, bad_cast, ios_base::failure, etc.
Draw stack frames: main -> func1 -> func2. When func2 throws, show arrows going back up with X through func2 objects, then into func1 catch block.

analogies.analogy: Like a security guard (throw) spotting a problem and raising alarm, then a manager (catch) handling the specific issue based on what went wrong.
Like evacuating a building floor by floor - each floor (function) secures its area (calls destructors) before moving to the next level.
Basic: leave room as you found it but don't remember exact state. Strong: leave room exactly as before. Nothrow: never even make a mess.
Like catching a fragile object - catching by value means making a copy that might break, catching by reference keeps the original intact.
